query: Which React Hooks have you used, and when would you choose each?

competitors.label: React Hooks for Interviews
competitors.selectionReason: This is the direct GreatFrontEnd comparator for hook selection, common pitfalls, and interview framing.
theirStrengths: React Hooks for Interviews is a direct, credible comparator for this topic.
theirGaps: React Hooks for Interviews is less compressed around the specific interview framing and likely follow-up confusion this page targets.

realWorldUseCases:
Decision-first answer

A strong interview answer is not just “I used useState, useEffect, useRef...” It is “I choose hooks by job.” Use useState for simple local UI state, useReducer when many transitions belong to one state machine, useRef for mutable values that should not trigger renders, and useEffect only when syncing with something outside React.
actionableExamples: This component uses useState because the search text is simple local UI state, useReducer because filter changes are event-driven and related, useDeferredValue because typing should stay urgent while large-list filtering can lag slightly, useMemo because filtering is derived and potentially expensive, and useRef plus useEffect because focusing an input is a DOM side effect rather than render state.
followUpConfusion:
High-signal trade-offs interviewers expect
  • useState vs useReducer: use reducer when transitions belong together and event names make the flow easier to reason about.
  • useRef vs state: refs store mutable values without rerendering; state exists for values the UI should reflect.
  • useEffect: reach for it when syncing with the outside world, not for values you can derive during render.
  • useMemo/useCallback: optimize only when they protect measurable work or prop stability.

realWorldUseCases: React Hooks for Interviews is also strong on hook selection framing, so this axis is competitive rather than a clean win.
actionableExamples: React Hooks for Interviews includes strong examples, but our rewritten page now includes a clearer component-level example tied directly to hook choice.
followUpConfusion: React Hooks for Interviews is less direct on the likely follow-up confusion than our shipped answer.

realWorldUseCases: Keep the decision-first first fold, but no immediate rewrite is needed because this axis is already competitive.
actionableExamples: No immediate rewrite follow-up is needed on the example axis after the component-level hook-choice example.

competitors.winReason: React Hooks for Interviews remains a hard comparator, but our page now earns a real edge on example clarity and follow-up trade-off handling while staying competitive on the general use-case axis.
